notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Based on your profile as a self-employed UX consultant in Austin filing Schedule C with no employer benefits, this question has a fairly clear answer—but both coverages matter and ideally you'd build toward having both.
Disability insurance should come first
Your income depends entirely on your ability to work. As a solo consultant, if illness or injury sidelines you for weeks or months, there's no employer salary continuation, no short-term disability plan, and no paycheck from clients who aren't being served. Looking at your May transactions, your income came from three separate client payments—Figma Design Systems ($8,500), Notion UX Research ($4,200), and a Stripe deposit ($1,260)—totaling $13,960 in a single month. That's a meaningful income stream that would simply stop if you couldn't work.
Since insurance decisions involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a licensed insurance professional for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
For UX consultants, long-term disability (LTD) is typically designed around a few key features worth knowing:
Own-occupation definition: This matters significantly for knowledge workers. It means you're considered disabled if you can't perform your specific work as a UX consultant, not just any work.
Elimination period: This is the waiting period before benefits begin—commonly 60, 90, or 180 days. Since you're self-employed, choosing one that aligns with your emergency fund is important. A 90-day elimination period is commonly used by freelancers with a modest cash cushion.
Benefit period: Ideally runs to age 65 for maximum protection, though shorter periods reduce premiums.
Income replacement range: Planning discussions commonly illustrate 60–70% of income as a target range. This is an educational illustration only—not a recommendation. An insurance agent can calculate the right amount for your situation.
Professional liability is a close second
As a UX consultant, you regularly give specialized advice that clients rely on for product decisions. If a client claims your work caused financial loss—a flawed design system, missed deliverables, or disputed recommendations—professional liability (also called errors & omissions, or E&O) insurance covers your defense costs and potential settlements. Even an unfounded claim can be expensive to defend.
The urgency of E&O rises if any of your contracts include indemnification clauses, or if larger enterprise clients (like the type that might engage Figma or Notion-scale projects) begin requiring proof of coverage as a condition of engagement. A licensed insurance professional can help you understand what limits make sense given your typical project sizes and client types.
Practical sequencing for your situation
Given that you live alone with no dependents and purchase marketplace health insurance individually, your income is your primary financial asset—making disability protection the more acute gap right now. I'd suggest exploring both coverages simultaneously through quotes, then prioritizing disability if budget requires you to phase the two.
One additional note: you're already contributing to an HSA through Fidelity, which is a smart move. Disability benefits, depending on how the policy is structured, could interact with your tax picture as a Schedule C filer—so a licensed agent familiar with self-employed clients would be well positioned to help you structure both coverages efficiently.
Would you like to explore what your emergency fund situation looks like relative to choosing an elimination period for disability coverage?
